line_text: What all these deals have in common is that the sellers weren’t advised by Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, or JP Morgan. These global banks lost some of the largest tech M&A mandates to a small but mighty elite boutique investment bank founded by the legendary Frank Quattrone at the height of the 2008 financial crisis. This is the story of Qatalyst Partners - The Most Feared Bank in Silicon Valley

line_text: Today, we'll be going into how one man made a generational bet that catapulted him to tech banking royalty and how he came back after being banned on Wall Street to building the most dominant tech M&A firm on the planet.

assets.description: Investment bankers at work in the office
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Investment bankers at work in the office

line_text: It’s a story that involves Steve Jobs, a prison sentence, and lots and lots of sly deal making and what you’ll learn is that the most dangerous person in the room isn’t the one who knows a little about everything - it’s the one who has gone so deep on one thing that there’s simply no one else to call

line_text: Upon graduating in 1977, his first job out of school is at Morgan Stanley in New York and back then, Morgan Stanley ran a different version of the 2+2 where you work for two years, get an MBA, and then come back.

line_text: Quattrone takes the deal, goes to Stanford for business school, and that Stanford-to-Silicon Valley pipeline defines the rest of his career.

line_text: A year later, a 25-year-old Steve Jobs walks into Jack McDonald's Stanford investment class. Quattrone is in the room and basically gets a glimpse into the future when Steve Jobs pitches the future of personal computing andQuattrone is hooked. So much so that he bets his entire career on it.

line_text: After graduating from Stanford, in 1981 Quattrone returns to Morgan Stanley and practically begs to join its San Francisco office which his New York colleagues thought was career suicide given the SF office had just opened up and had literally zero revenue

line_text: But as we know now, Quattrone’s move wasn’t a foolish but a generational bet as he wouldn’t just predict Silicon's Valley’s rise - he would help cause it

assets.transcript_excerpt: the grueling hours of investment banking
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Working late on deal execution

line_text: In some sense though, Quattrone was 9 years too early because his first notable deal came when he took Cisco public in 1990 and then Intuit in 1993

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121

line_text: But the world would change forever in 1995 when Quattrone led Netscape’s IPO, which was originally set to be offered at $14 per share and opened at $71, becoming a $2.9 billion company with zero profits and kicking off the dot.com bubble that would burst 5 years later. The Internet had arrived, and Quattrone was the one who rang the bell

line_text: By 1996, Quattrone had built Morgan Stanley’s tech group from nothing to its largest industry group at the firm even advising on Apple’s iconic acquisition of NeXT which brought Steve Jobs back to Apple.

line_text: But Quattrone felt he wasn’t being compensated fairly despite making $1 million more than Morgan Stanley’s chairman and so lured by a 3 year $20 million deal plus incentives, Quattrone decided to take his talents to Deutsche Bank, creating a mass exodus of bankers - a trend that you’ll see happen continuously - and took Amazon public in 1997 but the joyous marriage doesn’t last long

line_text: While Deutsche Bank pulled back its Sillicon Valley ambitions, Credit Suisse First Boston gave Quattrone an offer he couldn’t refuse: a shot at building his own kingdom.

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121

line_text: So in 1998, Quattrone joins CSFB, bringing along with him over 150 bankers and support staff, gutting Deutsche Bank’s entire tech practice so fast that Deutsche Bank simply sold its building to CSFB so Quattrone and his team didn’t even need to move offices

assets.description: Backup for broll line 19: Deutsche Bank tower

assets.match_reasoning: Image of Deutsche Bank tower matching script content

line_text: From 1998 to 2000, Quattrone almost single-handedly helps boost CSFB’s underwriting rank in IPOs from 8 in 1998 to number 1 in 2000 and tripling fees from $203 million to $686 million, and CSFB leads more tech IPOs than Goldman or Morgan Stanley combined.

line_text: Among them is the most insane IPO in history - the VA Linux IPO, which jumped from $30 to over $200 within minutes, a 700% increase.

assets.video_title: Wall Street's $5 Trillion Scam: The Truth About the Dot-Com Bubble.
assets.description: Dot-com bubble footage with stock tickers soaring

line_text: In the year 2000, Quattrone earns $120 million in a single year and he reportedly threw some crazy all-night parties and is crowned the Prince of Sillicon Valley, a gatekeeper to the world’s top tech IPOs and riding this high is exactly what gets him into trouble when the dot-com bubble bursts

assets.video_title: Wall Street's $5 Trillion Scam: The Truth About the Dot-Com Bubble.
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about dot com crash: Markets crashing, traders in panic

line_text: Transition: SECTION 2: From Tech Royalty to Prison Time

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Goldman Sachs headquarters exterior

line_text: Suddenly, the same practices that made Quattrone rich are under attack in the press, causing him to go from his absolute peak to a prison sentence

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Investment banking pitch process

line_text: After the dot com bubble bursts, companies go from billions to near zero, and everyone is trying to look for someone to blame and who better than the prince of Silicon Valley

assets.description: Goldman Sachs deal team at work
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Goldman Sachs deal team at work

line_text: It’s a well known secret that Quattrone had been handing out hot tech IPO share allocations that were guaranteed to make money on day one to key executives who could help sway their company’s decision to use CSFB as their investment bank and this group was known as “Friends of Frank”

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121
assets.description: Quattrone on the IPO allocation controversy
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about friends of frank: Quattrone on the IPO allocation controversy

assets.video_title: The Untouchables (full documentary) | FRONTLINE
assets.description: Documentary footage on Wall Street fraud investigations
assets.transcript_excerpt: prosecutors were looking at every major firm
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about friends of frank: Documentary footage on Wall Street fraud investigations

line_text: Interestingly, Quattrone is never charged for that shady conduct, and CSFB pays $100 million in a settlement with the SEC for profit-sharing arrangements, so here’s what leads to his ultimate arrest.

assets.description: Goldman Sachs MD discussing deal philosophy
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Goldman Sachs MD discussing deal philosophy

line_text: On December 3, 2000, CSFB's general counsel David Brodsky emails Quattrone about ongoing SEC and grand jury investigations into the firm's IPO allocation process and tells him not to tell anyone.

assets.description: MD on what makes a great dealmaker
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: MD on what makes a great dealmaker

line_text: The next day, Richard Char, one of Quattrone's colleagues, sends an email suggesting the tech group do some year-end “file cleaning” — standard housekeeping under CSFB's document retention policy. Quattrone replies: "You shouldn't make jokes like that on email!"

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Wall Street financial district

line_text: The next day on December 5th, Quattrone sends his own email to the group, writing: "Having been a key witness in a securities litigation case in south texas,I strongly advise you to follow these procedures." and these 22 words becomes the basis for his prosecution.

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121
assets.transcript_excerpt: the hardest period of my life
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about quattrone trial: Quattrone discussing his legal battles

line_text: The prosecution argues that Quattrone knew about the grand jury subpoena and forwarded a memo encouraging documentation destruction, and therefore corruptly obstructing justice

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Banking industry overview footage

line_text: In February 2003, Quattrone is suspendedfrom CSFB but he doesn’t seem too bothered by it seeing as he plays golf with Bill Murray and Kevin Costner days later

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Financial industry networking

line_text: But in April 2003, Quattrone is arrested by the FBI with charges brought by US Attorney Jim Comey - the same one who became the FBI director.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Financial markets and trading

line_text: In October 2003, the first trial ends in a hung jury that’s deadlocked after 5 days of deliberation, in May 2004 he’s convicted on 3 counts, and in September 2004, he’s sentenced to 18 months in prison in part for also lying on the witness stand and also given a lifetime ban by the NASD from the securities industry

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Wall Street regulatory environment

line_text: Quattrone appeals the sentence and long-story short after many years of legal battles, all chargse are dropped in 2007, CSFB is ordered to pay him up to $550 million in overdue compensation, and the most powerful tech banker in the world is finally allowed to start working again but the empire he had built is gone

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Banking industry competition

line_text: Most people who fall this far don't come back, but here’s the thing about Quattrone: his superpower was never just deal execution. It was making people feel like the most important person in the room, and in cinematic timing, Quattrone moves from one market crash to the next by launching Qatalyst in 2008 during the Great Recession and within days, gets a phone call from Google .

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Explaining investment banking roles

line_text: SECTION 3: The Improbable Comeback

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Deal execution process explained

line_text: The timing looks insane in hindsight. Bear Stearns has just been sold to JPMorgan for $2 a share — down from $150 a year earlier. Lehman falls six months later. M&A freezes globally and the entire banking industry is in shambles.

assets.video_title: Money, Power and Wall Street, Part One (full documentary) | FRONTLINE
assets.transcript_excerpt: Bear Stearns was sold for $2 a share
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about bear stearns: Bear Stearns collapse footage

assets.video_title: Why Warren Buffett Said No to Lehman and AIG in 2008
assets.transcript_excerpt: the entire banking system was on the brink
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about bear stearns: Analysis of 2008 bank failures

line_text: But Quattrone's rolodex is fully intact. Within days of the founding press release going out, Google reaches out. They need someone to help them create an alternative to Microsoft's $45 billion hostile takeover bid for Yahoo. The firm hasn't even closed its first deal yet and Google is on the phone.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Market analysis and predictions

line_text: That first year, Quattrone doesn't even know if they can make payroll but manages to pull off his first deal and earn fees on December 25th, 2008. Literally Christmas Day.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Economic cycles and crashes

line_text: But their first true breakout deal is Data Domain's sale to EMC in 2009. Qatalyst runs the process, engineers a bidding war and nearly doubles the purchase price from an original $1.5 billion offer from NetApp to $2.4 billion by the end of it

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about data domain: Quattrone on early Qatalyst deals

line_text: That's followed by 3PAR. In 2010, Dell makes an initial bid of $1.15 billion and Qatalyst runs another competitive process, driving the ultimate acquisition price to $2.4 billion with the winning bid from HP.

assets.video_title: News Update: 3PAR Accepted Dell's Upped Offer Of $24.30 Per Share August 26, 2010
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about 3par deal: 3PAR bidding war news coverage

line_text: And here's where there’s some tea with HP. Later in October 2011, Qatalyst runs a process for Autonomy which is sold to HP for $10.3 billion which after a CEO transition is inherited by Meg Whitman.

assets.video_title: HP/Autonomy: who knew?
assets.transcript_excerpt: who knew about the accounting problems at Autonomy
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about autonomy scandal: HP Autonomy controversy coverage

assets.video_title: HP Chairman Faces Fury Over Autonomy Acquisition
assets.description: HP Chairman faces fury over Autonomy acquisition
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about autonomy scandal: HP Chairman faces fury over Autonomy acquisition

assets.match_reasoning: Image of HP Meg Whitman headquarters matching script content

assets.description: Backup for broll line 43: HP Meg Whitman headquarters
assets.match_reasoning: Image of HP Meg Whitman headquarters matching script content

line_text: Barely a year after the deal, HP announces an $8.8 billion writedown of the Autonomy deal due to accounting fraud and Whitman publicly turns on Qatalyst, saying the firm was "guilty" of playing too aggressively.

assets.video_title: An 'Autonomy' of a Scandal - Accrual World S1 E2
assets.transcript_excerpt: the biggest acquisition disaster in tech history
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about autonomy scandal: Autonomy scandal documentary analysis

assets.description: Backup for broll line 43: HP Meg Whitman headquarters
assets.match_reasoning: Image of HP Meg Whitman headquarters matching script content

line_text: She hates Qatalyst so much that when the bank was running a sale process for Aruba Networks, Whitman could only negotiate by bringing in a buffer person to sit between Whitman and Qatalyst so the two sides never had to directly face each other. The deal amazingly still closed at $2.7BN with HP as the buyer even with Whitman believing George Boutros to be “evil.”

assets.video_title: Negotiating Advice From the Man Behind Tech’s BIGGEST Acquisitions (LinkedIn, Slack, Figma)
assets.transcript_excerpt: every deal is different, but the principles are the same
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about boutros deals: Boutros discussing major deal execution

assets.description: George Boutros - George Boutros Qatalyst banker
assets.match_reasoning: Image of George Boutros matching script content

assets.description: George Boutros - George Boutros Qatalyst banker
assets.match_reasoning: Image of George Boutros matching script content

line_text: And so this is a great time for us to talk about the 2nd most important person in Qatalyst’s history - George Boutros

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Financial crime investigations

line_text: Boutros joined Qatalyst in 2010, coming from Credit Suisse, where he led the sales of YouTube to Google and Pixar to Disney.

assets.video_title: Negotiating Advice From the Man Behind Tech’s BIGGEST Acquisitions (LinkedIn, Slack, Figma)
assets.description: George Boutros discussing his dealmaking philosophy
assets.transcript_excerpt: you have to be willing to walk away
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about boutros intro: George Boutros discussing his dealmaking philosophy

line_text: Quattrone and Boutros are a powerful duo that is known for their good cop, bad cop personas. Quattrone’s reputation is all about relationships — warm, client-facing, and making you feel heard. Boutro’s reputation is notorious for being brash, aggressive, and execution-focused.

assets.video_title: Negotiating Advice From the Man Behind Tech’s BIGGEST Acquisitions (LinkedIn, Slack, Figma)
assets.description: Boutros on his reputation for aggressive negotiations
assets.transcript_excerpt: I push hard because my clients deserve the best price
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about boutros aggressive: Boutros on his reputation for aggressive negotiations

line_text: Quattrone is the optimist. Boutros is the pessimist. On client calls, Quattrone tells you to shoot for the moon. Boutros brings you back with risk-adjusted reality. Clients get both shoulders of the advice and that polarizing dynamic sets the foundation for Qatalyst’s dominance in the years that followed.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Prosecution of financial crimes

line_text: i After just one year of Boutros joining, things really start to fly in 2011. Motorola to Google - $12.5 billion. Autonomy to HP - $10.3 billion. National Semiconductor to TI - $6.5BN. Riot Games to Tencent and so many more all in a single year with a team of only 20 bankers so you know those bonuses were fat and at this point the question isn’t if Qatalyst can compete with Goldman and Morgan Stanley - it’s whether the big banks can compete with Qatalyst

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Courtroom proceedings

line_text: By this point, Quattrone is hailed as an all-time rainmaker and takes Qatalyst to new heights by advising Dell’s $24.4 billion take-private transaction and in 2015, Qatalyst advises on 12 deals worth $33.5 billion, capturing 7% of total tech deal volume with only an 80 person firm

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone reflecting on career

line_text: Then suddenly in 2016 like Michael Jordan’s retirement at the peak of his powers, Quattrone steps down as CEO and hands over day to day operations to Boutros, which is an important step in establishing Qatalyst as an institution with a succession structure, not just a personality cult around one man and this is when Qatalyst goes on a tear making it the most feared tech bank bully on Wall Street

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on deal strategy

line_text: SECTION 4: From Underdog to Bully

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on building a firm

line_text: With Quattrone still deeply involved in deals, Qatalyst goes on a tear and for those of you who are deal junkies, you’re going to love the 3 deals I’m about to detail that showcase why Qatalyst went from being a dominant boutique to a nightmare to its competitors

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on client relationships

line_text: First is LinkedIn’s $26 billion sale to Microsoft in 2016 which resulted in one major bank disseminating a 10 page presentation to counteract Qatalyst, who was deemed Sillicon Valley’s Favorite Bully

assets.description: Backup for broll line 5: LinkedIn Microsoft deal
assets.match_reasoning: Image of LinkedIn Microsoft deal matching script content

line_text: But besides this being Qatalyst’s largest deal at the time, what makes this deal so fascinating is the baked in revenge story

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on market insights

line_text: In April 2016 just months before the LinkedIn deal would be announced, Quattrone tweeted about Credit Suisse throwing a top performer under the bus making it clear he still felt he was treated unfairly by the bank

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on competitive advantage

line_text: The greater context behind this is that Credit Suisse used to be Sillicon Valley’s bank in the 2000s but Quattrone had lured away Boutros who brought all his relationships with him as Credit Suisse’s Chairman of Tech banking

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on legacy and future

line_text: The LinkedIn deal was the defining deal of the era that showcased that Quattrone rebuilt the exact tech banking franchise that Credit Suisse took away from him and his tweet was just the cherry on top

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Boutros on deal mechanics

line_text: The second deal is AppDynamics, which was supposed to be the first big tech IPO of 2017 with Goldman, Morgan Stanley, and JP Morgan as the top 3 underwriters

assets.video_title: Why Cisco Wants to Buy AppDynamics for $3.7 Billion
assets.transcript_excerpt: Cisco pulled the trigger on a $3.7 billion deal
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about appdynamics deal: Analysis of Cisco buying AppDynamics

assets.video_title: AppDynamics Acquired for $3.7 Billion | Crunch Report
assets.transcript_excerpt: AppDynamics acquired for $3.7 billion just hours before IPO
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about appdynamics deal: AppDynamics acquisition breaking news

line_text: The IPO roadshow was going well but at the same time, the company had tapped Qatalyst to simultaneously run a sale and deliberately chose them to avoid info about the deal leaking to the press

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Boutros on client loyalty

line_text: In just 72 hours, Qatalyst sold the company to Cisco for $3.7 billion - 20% higher than the expected post IPO price and Qatalyst brilliantly used the pressure of the IPO as leverage for negotiations

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Boutros on industry evolution

line_text: This meant AppDynamics’ CEO was pitching the stock to investors hours before the acquisition’s announcement, which sent shock waves throughout the industry

assets.description: Boutros on future of boutique banking
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Boutros on future of boutique banking

line_text: Senior bankers pride themselves and win business by having deep relationships with their clients and during IPO roadshows are literally in rooms and planes with the CEO and CFO every day, so I’m sure the bankers leading the IPO were pissed because it means pennies for all their hard work while Qatalyst swooped in and collection tens of millions in fees

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Commentary on Quattrone success

line_text: Qatalyst was becoming every top tech firm’s preferred tech advisor and by 2019, Morgan Stanley and other top banks began pitching M&A IPO dual mandates during IPO conversations so Qatalyst couldn’t slide in and take away another deal, which is incredible since the biggest bank in tech is building its pitch around a 90 person boutique bank

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Analysis of Quattrone career

line_text: The last deal is Qualtrics’ sale to SAP, which is a masterclass in dealmaking. Quattrone invites both Ryan Smith — the CEO of Qualtrics — and Bill McDermott — the CEO of SAP — to speak back to back at Qatalyst’s annual Pebble Beach retreat so they could essentially inspire one another.

assets.video_title: Negotiating Advice From the Man Behind Tech’s BIGGEST Acquisitions (LinkedIn, Slack, Figma)
assets.description: Boutros on deals born from relationships
assets.transcript_excerpt: sometimes the best deals happen over coffee
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about qualtrics deal: Boutros on deals born from relationships

line_text: The two men disappear to a coffee shop mid-retreat and Quattrone joins them later and instructs that each of them give each other three reasons of why a merger would work. He steps away for an hour and comes back to a cocktail napkin with the deal terms sketched out and just like that an $8 billion deal is live.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on exciting tech sectors

line_text: What makes this deal crazy is that Goldman and Morgan Stanley were lead banks for Qualtrics’ IPO meaning they had deep relationships with the company but had absolutely zero idea about the sale and learned about it on the news like everyone else

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on tech investment trends

line_text: These transactions turn Qatalyst into the most feared bank in Silicon Valley as they’re able to pitch to every company that they consistently get higher premiums on billion-dollar sell-side deals, capture the highest revenue multiples in tech M&A, and dominate market share on the biggest transactions by a significant margin.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone at Web 2.0 Summit

line_text: So after looking back at all of this, what is the secret sauce? What is that actually makes Qatalyst so dominant?

assets.video_title: Negotiating Advice From the Man Behind Tech’s BIGGEST Acquisitions (LinkedIn, Slack, Figma)
assets.transcript_excerpt: we only do one thing and we do it better than anyone
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about secret sauce: Boutros on Qatalysts competitive advantage

assets.video_title: Frank Quattrone: Lessons from 650 M&A Deals Worth Over $1TRN & Taking Amazon and Cisco Public| E1121
assets.transcript_excerpt: focus is the key to everything
assets.match_reasoning: Matches script line about secret sauce: Quattrone on focus and specialization

line_text: SECTION 5: The Secret Sauce

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone discussing tech industry

line_text: After speaking with 2 Qatalyst bankers, I’ve learned that the firm’s dominance boils down to three main reasons

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on business exits

line_text: First is the fact that they focus on one thing - tech sell-side M&A and as Bruce Lee said - I do not fear the man who has practiced 10,000 kicks one, but I fear the man who has practiced one kick 10,000 times

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Quattrone on market conditions

line_text: Every big bank is trying to win IPO mandates, serve corporate buyers, manage lending relationships, and advising sellers

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Dot-com era stock market footage

line_text: [George Boutros interview b-roll - "The big banks are trying to serve too many stakeholders at once. When the sell-side advisor and the buy-side advisor are both the same bank, that's not advisory. That's conflict."]

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Internet company valuations soaring

line_text: While banks are practicing 10,000 different kicks, Qatalyst extracts the highest possible price from the buyer and has done so 10,000 times, with one spokesperson even saying "Our advice is never clouded by the fear that if we push for the highest price we might offend the buyer"

assets.transcript_excerpt: the wreckage of the dot-com crash
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Dot-com aftermath

line_text: So when the top tech companies want to sell, they know Qatalyst will get them the best price

assets.description: The Big Short financial drama scene
assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: The Big Short financial drama scene

line_text: Second is the network built over decades that has compounded over time with Quattrone and Boutrous at the helm

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Stock market crash footage

line_text: Every time Qatalyst closes a deal, they learn something no one else knows - how Microsoft negotiates under pressure, what Google’s internal approval process looks like, or where Amazon’s ceiling is

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Financial crisis market panic

line_text: Quattrone in particular has seen it all - from helping Apple buy Next to ringing in the dotcom era to dominating software sell-side M&A when you’re a client, you know you’re getting insight you can’t get anywhere else

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Great Depression historical footage

line_text: Lastly, there’s  the senior attention that scales down, not up

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Economic recovery footage

line_text: At a bulge bracket, deal size determines who shows up. A $2 billion transaction may get a senior MD while a $20 billion transaction gets a vice chairman

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Tech industry innovation showcase

line_text: At Qatalyst, the model inverts: CEO George Boutros is personally in every deal above $1 billion, screen-sharing presentations on client calls, in the numbers himself, and giving first-week analysts direct feedback.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: AI and tech future discussion

line_text: When the person who closes the deal is the same person who ran the last three deals for the same buyer, information doesn't get lost in translation.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Silicon Valley tech headquarters

line_text: These 3 factors are what has helped Qatalyst rise to the top. In 2023, Qatalyst announced $61 billion across 9 deals and in 2024, $95 billion across 14, leading to averages of $6.8 billion per deal while for context, Goldman’s value per deal hovers around $3 billion despite having a much larger team and more resources

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Tech company operations

line_text: OUTRO

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Steve Ballmer energetic presentation

line_text: Qatalyst proves that the biggest firm or doing the most doesn't always win. Whether you’re a student or a business owner, at some point, you’ll come across a fork in the road where you need to choose between chasing more opportunities and spreading your eggs across multiple baskets or sticking to what you know, honing your craft and practicing that one kick 10,000 times. If Qatalyst’s story teaches you anything, it’s that once you find your passion, you need to pick the latter because less is actually more.

assets.match_reasoning: Supporting visual: Corporate leadership and strategy

line_text: Hope you enjoyed this deep dive into Qatalyst. I actually resonate a lot with Quattrone’s story because for the past 5+ years of working on this channel, my main focus has been helping students break into investment banking and I’ve helped students break into firms like Qatalyst, Goldman, and every top bank. I’ve honed this craft of helping students for years now and [add plug]
